serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of Asam Curry Seaweed Beancurd Slices Rice weighs approximately 450 g, comprising a mixed grain base (red rice, brown rice, quinoa) topped with seaweed beancurd slices and asam curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore — Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — component cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; U.S. Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB or USDA entry exists for Asam Curry Seaweed Beancurd Slices Rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from the input record (GrabFood listing, ai_generated provenance, confidence 0.7) and cross-referenced against HPB Singapore component data for curry rice dishes, brown rice, tofu, and seaweed, with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as a secondary cross-check for beancurd (USDA #16126) and seaweed (USDA #11667) components. The HPB Foodo database (https://www.hpb.gov.sg/healthy-living/food-beverage/food-nutritional-composition) is the primary approved regional source. Values are retained from the input as the best available estimate; confidence is set to medium.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Asam Curry Seaweed Beancurd Slices Rice. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous mixed-grain rice dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed tables: brown rice GI ~50–55 (University of Sydney GI Database entry), red rice GI ~55 (Atkinson et al. 2021), quinoa GI ~53 (Atkinson et al. 2021). The curry sauce and beancurd components are low-GI and do not materially raise the composite GI. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (75.0 g from 450 g × 16.67 g/100g) / 100 = 41.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 266.67 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, approaching 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt curry paste and limit portion size to 250–300 g per meal; the whole-grain base (brown rice, red rice, quinoa) and seaweed provide potassium and fibre that partially offset sodium load, but sodium content remains a primary concern at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~55) and moderate carbohydrate (16.67 g/100 g); a 450 g serving provides approximately 75 g carbohydrate, which requires careful portion management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: The mixed-grain base (brown rice, red rice, quinoa) provides a lower GI than white rice and the 2.22 g/100 g dietary fibre and 5.56 g/100 g protein from beancurd help moderate postprandial glucose rise; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit serving to 250–300 g and monitor total carbohydrate intake per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.0 g/100 g from coconut milk; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 18 g saturated fat, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 20 g for many adults.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The whole-grain rice base and seaweed provide beneficial fibre and minerals supporting cardiovascular health, but the coconut milk-based curry contributes significant saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced coconut milk, limit portion to 250 g, and note that cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g and trans fat is negligible at 0.02 g/100 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Multiple nightshade vegetables (eggplant, tomato, chilli) present in the curry; solanine-containing ingredients may aggravate joint inflammation in nightshade-sensitive RA individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and known nightshade sensitivity should avoid or request modification of this dish to exclude eggplant, tomato, and chilli; anti-inflammatory components including turmeric, ginger, lemongrass, and seaweed are beneficial and should be retained.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Asam Curry Seaweed Beancurd Slices Rice is a Singaporean mixed-grain rice dish featuring a tangy, tamarind-based (asam) curry sauce served over a nutritious blend of red rice, brown rice, and quinoa, topped with seaweed-infused beancurd (tofu) slices. The curry is prepared by simmering a spiced paste of lemongrass, turmeric, ginger, chilli, and tamarind with coconut milk, producing a rich, sour-spicy sauce characteristic of Peranakan and Southeast Asian asam curry traditions. The rice base is boiled or steamed and provides a chewy, nutty texture from the whole grains and quinoa. The beancurd slices absorb the curry flavours and contribute a soft, silken texture, while the seaweed adds umami depth and a slight oceanic note. Nutritionally, the dish provides 144 kcal per 100 g, with 5.56 g protein, 6.22 g fat (4.0 g saturated from coconut milk), and 16.67 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Dietary fibre is 2.22 g/100 g, supporting digestive health. Sodium is 266.67 mg/100 g, which is moderate-to-high and requires monitoring for hypertension. The mixed-grain base offers a lower glycaemic index (~55) compared to white rice, making it a more suitable option for individuals managing type 2 diabetes when portion-controlled. The dish is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, and halal dietary requirements. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course in Singapore hawker centres and health-conscious food delivery platforms.

GI estimated at 55 from analogous mixed-grain rice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed tables (Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632).
